1: ἐπιμένωμεν τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ ἵνα ἡ χάρις πλεονάσῃ (epimenōmen tē hamartia hina hē charis pleonasē) — "shall we continue in sin so that grace may increase?" The logical inference from 5:20 stated as the objection Paul anticipated. Epimenō (to remain upon, persist in) — the deliberate, continued choice to remain in sin, not accidental failure. The mē genoito (certainly not!) that follows does not argue against the inference; it demonstrates the absurdity of what the inference requires one to overlook.
2: οἵτινες ἀπεθάνομεν τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ (hoitines apethanomen tē hamartia) — "we who died to sin" — aorist active: a completed death. The relative clause hoitines (we who are such people that) identifies the believers by this death-event. πῶς ἔτι ζήσομεν ἐν αὐτῇ — "how shall we still live in it?" — the rhetorical question points to the ontological impossibility: the dead person cannot continue in the life they died out of.
3: ἐβαπτίσθημεν εἰς Χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν (ebaptisthēmen eis Christon Iēsoun) — "were baptized into Christ Jesus" — the eis (into) is significant: baptism is the act of going-into-Christ, of union with his person. εἰς τὸν θάνατον αὐτοῦ ἐβαπτίσθημεν — "baptized into his death" — the union is specifically with the death-event; baptism does not merely commemorate Christ's death but unites the baptized person with it.
4: συνετάφημεν οὖν αὐτῷ διὰ τοῦ βαπτίσματος (synetaphēmen oun autō dia tou baptismatos) — "we were buried therefore with him through baptism" — syntaptō (to bury together with) — a compound verb of solidarity: not just that Christ was buried but that we were buried with him. ἐν καινότητι ζωῆς (en kainotēti zōēs) — "in newness of life" — kainotēs (newness in kind, qualitative novelty) as opposed to neos (new in age/time); the resurrection life is not the old life renewed but an ontologically new mode of existence.
5: σύμφυτοι γεγόναμεν τῷ ὁμοιώματι τοῦ θανάτου αὐτοῦ (symphytoi gegonamen tō homoiōmati tou thanatou autou) — "we have been united with the likeness of his death." Symphytos (grown-together-with, organically joined) — a horticultural term (cf. grafting): the believer is not merely alongside Christ's death as a moral example but organically united to it. The perfect gegonamen (we have become and remain) indicates permanent organic union.
6: ὁ παλαιὸς ἡμῶν ἄνθρωπος συνεσταυρώθη (ho palaios hēmōn anthrōpos synestaurōthē) — "our old man was crucified together with him" — palaios anthrōpos (old humanity) — not the body but the previous Adam-shaped mode of existence. Systauroō (to crucify together with) — the compound form of crucifixion: co-crucifixion, union in the death event. ἵνα καταργηθῇ τὸ σῶμα τῆς ἁμαρτίας — "so that the body of sin might be rendered inoperative" — katargeō again: not destroyed but rendered powerless, inactivated.
7: ὁ γὰρ ἀποθανὼν δεδικαίωται ἀπὸ τῆς ἁμαρτίας (ho gar apothanōn dedikaiōtai apo tēs hamartias) — "for the one who has died has been justified from sin." The perfect passive dedikaiōtai: has been and remains acquitted from sin's claims. Death severs all legal claims; the dead person owes no further obligations to the old master. This principle Paul applies: the believer who has died with Christ is no longer subject to sin's legal authority.
8: πιστεύομεν ὅτι καὶ συζήσομεν αὐτῷ (pisteuomen hoti kai syzēsomen autō) — "we believe that we will also live together with him." The future tense syzēsomen (we will co-live) points to the future resurrection as the completion of the union initiated in baptism; but the present tense pisteuomen (we are believing) grounds the future hope in present faith rather than future achievement.
9: Χριστὸς ἐγερθεὶς ἐκ νεκρῶν οὐκέτι ἀποθνῄσκει — "Christ, having been raised from the dead, no longer dies." Egertheisidēs (aorist passive participle: having been raised) describes the historical event; apothnēskei (present tense: dies) the ongoing state — he no longer dies. θάνατος αὐτοῦ οὐκέτι κυριεύει — "death no longer lords it over him" — kyrieuō (to be lord, to exercise dominion) — the resurrection terminates death's lordship over Christ. Believers who are united with the risen Christ share in this freedom from death's dominion.
10: ὃ γὰρ ἀπέθανεν, τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ ἀπέθανεν ἐφάπαξ (ho gar apethanen, tē hamartia apethanen ephapax) — "for the death he died, he died to sin once for all." Ephapax (once for all, once for all time) — a term Paul uses only here and in Hebrews-related contexts; the finality and completeness of Christ's single death excludes repetition. ὃ δὲ ζῇ, ζῇ τῷ θεῷ — "but the life he lives, he lives to God" — the present tenses indicate the permanent quality of the resurrection life: it belongs entirely to God.
11: λογίζεσθε ἑαυτοὺς νεκροὺς μὲν τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ ζῶντας δὲ τῷ θεῷ (logizesthe heautous nekrous men tē hamartia zōntas de tō theō) — "reckon yourselves dead to sin but alive to God." Logizesthe (present active imperative: keep reckoning, continue counting) — the same forensic-accounting verb from ch4, now an ethical command. The command is not to make something true by effort but to count as true what is already true in Christ; the reckoning is the believing reception of the accomplished reality.
12: μὴ οὖν βασιλευέτω ἡ ἁμαρτία ἐν τῷ θνητῷ ὑμῶν σώματι (mē oun basileuetō hē hamartia en tō thnētō hymōn sōmati) — "let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body." The present imperative with mē can mean: stop what is in progress, or maintain the prevention of what might begin. Thnētō sōmati (mortal body) — sin's domain is the body subject to death; the body is not evil but is the site where sin seeks to re-establish its reign after it has been deposed in principle.
13: παραστάνετε ἑαυτοὺς τῷ θεῷ (parastanete heautous tō theō) — "present yourselves to God" — paristēmi (to place beside, present) has a cultic resonance: the priestly presentation of the offering. The believer presents themselves as living offerings (cf. 12:1). ὡσεὶ ἐκ νεκρῶν ζῶντας — "as those who are alive from the dead" — the ethical imperative is grounded in the indicative status: present yourselves as what you actually are — the risen-from-the-dead.
14: ἁμαρτία γὰρ ὑμῶν οὐ κυριεύσει (hamartia gar hymōn ou kyrieusei) — "for sin will not be lord over you" — future active: a categorical promise, not merely an aspiration. οὐ γάρ ἐστε ὑπὸ νόμον ἀλλ᾽ ὑπὸ χάριν — "for you are not under law but under grace" — the preposition hypo (under) indicates dominion: "under law" = subject to law's jurisdiction and condemnation; "under grace" = in grace's domain where sin has been dethroned.
15: τί οὖν; ἁμαρτήσωμεν ὅτι οὐκ ἐσμὲν ὑπὸ νόμον ἀλλ᾽ ὑπὸ χάριν — the second objection to Paul's grace-teaching; structurally parallel to 6:1 but from a different angle. v1 raised the possibility of continuing in sin under grace; v15 raises the possibility of sinning once because under grace rather than law. The mē genoito dismisses both with equal finality.
16: ᾧ παριστάνετε ἑαυτοὺς δούλους εἰς ὑπακοήν — "to whom you present yourselves as slaves for obedience" — the slave-obedience framework: one always serves someone; freedom from sin is not freedom from all masters but transfer to a new master. ἤτοι ἁμαρτίας εἰς θάνατον ἢ ὑπακοῆς εἰς δικαιοσύνην — "either of sin leading to death or of obedience leading to righteousness" — the binary is unavoidable: one's obedience determines one's destiny-direction.
17: ὑπηκούσατε δὲ ἐκ καρδίας εἰς ὃν παρεδόθητε τύπον διδαχῆς (hypēkousate de ek kardias eis hon paredothēte typon didachēs) — "you obeyed from the heart the pattern of teaching to which you were handed over." Note the passive: the believers were delivered to the teaching, not just taught it; the teaching takes possession of its recipients. Ek kardias (from the heart) — the obedience is interior and voluntary, not merely behavioral compliance.
18: ἐλευθερωθέντες δὲ ἀπὸ τῆς ἁμαρτίας ἐδουλώθητε τῇ δικαιοσύνῃ — "having been freed from sin, you became enslaved to righteousness." Both ēleutherōthentes (freed) and edoulōthēte (enslaved) are aorist passives: both are divine acts, both completed. Freedom from sin is not independence but transfer of ownership — enslaved to righteousness as the new master.
19: ἀνθρώπινον λέγω διὰ τὴν ἀσθένειαν τῆς σαρκὸς ὑμῶν — "I am speaking in human terms because of the weakness of your flesh" — Paul acknowledges the inadequacy of the slave-metaphor while using it for pedagogical accessibility. The metaphor breaks down because Christian life under grace is both freedom and service simultaneously; the slave-image captures only the service dimension.
20: ἐλεύθεροι ἦτε τῇ δικαιοσύνῃ (eleutheroi ēte tē dikaiosynē) — "you were free from righteousness" — the dative of reference: free with respect to righteousness, i.e., righteousness had no claim on you. The irony is sharp: slavery to sin was experienced as freedom; the "freedom" was in fact maximum bondage.
21: τίνα οὖν καρπὸν εἴχετε τότε — "what fruit did you have then?" — the fruit metaphor measures the product of the old life: the karpos (fruit) of the sin-life is shame and death. The harvest of sin reveals its character; Paul invites the Romans to evaluate their pre-conversion life by its actual product.
22: εἰς ἁγιασμόν (eis hagiasmon) — "resulting in sanctification" — hagiasmos (holiness, sanctification, the process of becoming holy) — different from hagiasmos as an instantaneous act; the word indicates the ongoing process of being consecrated to God. τέλος δὲ ζωὴν αἰώνιον — "and the end/goal is eternal life" — telos (end, completion, goal) — the harvest-metaphor reaches its eschatological conclusion: eternal life is both the end-of-the-process and the final harvest.
23: τὰ γὰρ ὀψώνια τῆς ἁμαρτίας θάνατος (ta gar opsōnia tēs hamartias thanatos) — "for the wages of sin is death." Opsōnion (wage, ration, soldier's pay) — a military term for the wages due a soldier; sin pays its own workers exactly what it owes them. τὸ δὲ χάρισμα τοῦ θεοῦ ζωὴ αἰώνιος ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ τῷ κυρίῳ ἡμῶν — "but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord" — charisma (grace-gift) deliberately contrasted with opsōnion (earned wages): life is not a wage but a gift, not earned but given.
